label: GitHub
description: Connect to GitHub to read repositories, pull requests, issues, and commits.

form.title: Connect GitHub
form.subtitle: Authorize Anton to access your GitHub repositories, pull requests, and issues.

methods.label: In-Browser Connect
methods.description: Opens GitHub sign-in in your browser. No credentials required — just click Connect.

methods.label: Fine-grained personal access token
methods.description: Paste a fine-grained personal access token (github_pat_…) scoped to exactly the repositories and permissions you choose. Simplest and most secure path — no OAuth client setup required.
methods.how_to:
## Fine-grained personal access token

Fine-grained tokens let you scope access to specific repositories and only the permissions Anton needs — far safer than classic tokens.

### Steps

1. Go to [GitHub Settings](https://github.com/settings) → scroll down to **Developer settings** (bottom of the left sidebar).
2. Open **Personal access tokens → Fine-grained tokens**.
3. Click **Generate new token**.
4. Give it a name like `Anton` and set an expiration (90 days is a reasonable default).
5. Under **Resource owner**, pick your personal account or an org you belong to.
6. Under **Repository access**, choose **Only select repositories** and tick the repos Anton should see, or pick **All repositories**.
7. Under **Permissions → Repository permissions**, enable at minimum:
   - **Contents** → Read-only (for code and commits)
   - **Pull requests** → Read-only
   - **Issues** → Read-only
   - Add **Metadata** → Read-only (required automatically)
8. Click **Generate token**. GitHub shows the token once — copy it immediately.
9. Paste it into the **Personal access token** field below.

### Notes

- The token starts with `github_pat_`. If yours starts with `ghp_`, you generated a classic token instead — see the **Classic personal access token** method.
- Tokens expire. When one expires, generate a new one and update the field in Anton.
- If the repo is owned by an org, the org admin may need to approve the token under **Organization settings → Personal access tokens**.

fields.name: access_token
fields.label: Personal access token
fields.description: Fine-grained token from GitHub Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Fine-grained tokens. Shown only once at creation.

fields.name: base_url
fields.label: GitHub base URL
fields.description: Leave as the default for github.com. Change only if you use GitHub Enterprise Server (e.g. https://github.yourcompany.com).

methods.label: Classic personal access token
methods.description: Use a classic token (ghp_…) if fine-grained tokens are not available for your org or use case. Scopes are coarser-grained than fine-grained tokens.
methods.how_to:
## Classic personal access token

Classic tokens are the older GitHub auth mechanism. They still work but grant broader access than fine-grained tokens. Prefer fine-grained if your org supports them.

### Steps

1. Go to [GitHub Settings](https://github.com/settings) → **Developer settings** → **Personal access tokens → Tokens (classic)**.
2. Click **Generate new token (classic)**.
3. Give it a descriptive note like `Anton` and set an expiration.
4. Select the scopes Anton needs:
   - `repo` — full access to repositories (or `public_repo` for public repos only)
   - `read:org` — if you need org-level data
   - `read:user` — basic user info
5. Click **Generate token**. Copy the token (starts with `ghp_`) — it is shown only once.
6. Paste it into the **Personal access token** field below.

### Notes

- The `repo` scope grants write access as well as read. If Anton only needs to read, be aware there is no read-only sub-scope for classic tokens — use fine-grained tokens for narrower access.
- If your org requires SSO, click **Configure SSO** next to the token after generating it and authorize the org.

fields.name: access_token
fields.label: Personal access token (classic)
fields.description: Classic token from GitHub Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Tokens (classic). Shown only once at creation.

fields.name: base_url
fields.label: GitHub base URL
fields.description: Leave as the default for github.com. Change only if you use GitHub Enterprise Server.

methods.label: OAuth (GitHub App)
methods.description: OAuth 2.0 flow using your own GitHub OAuth App. Opens a browser window for GitHub authorization. Requires creating an OAuth App in GitHub Developer settings first.
methods.how_to:
## OAuth via GitHub OAuth App (BYOK)

This flow opens GitHub's consent page in your browser. You must first create a GitHub OAuth App and supply its credentials.

### Create a GitHub OAuth App

1. Go to [GitHub Settings](https://github.com/settings) → **Developer settings** → **OAuth Apps** → **New OAuth App**.
2. Fill in:
   - **Application name**: `Anton`
   - **Homepage URL**: `http://localhost`
   - **Authorization callback URL**: `http://localhost:9898/oauth/callback` (Anton uses this local redirect)
3. Click **Register application**.
4. On the app page, copy the **Client ID**.
5. Click **Generate a new client secret**, copy the value immediately — it is shown only once.
6. Paste both into the fields below, then click **Submit** to start the browser flow.

### Scopes requested

- `repo` — read and write access to repositories
- `read:org` — read org membership and teams
- `read:user` — read your public profile

### Notes

- GitHub OAuth tokens do not expire by default, but you can revoke them at **Settings → Applications → Authorized OAuth Apps**.
- For GitHub Enterprise Server, change the base URL field to your instance URL.

fields.name: client_id
fields.label: OAuth client ID
fields.description: From your GitHub OAuth App page under Developer settings → OAuth Apps.

fields.name: client_secret
fields.label: OAuth client secret
fields.description: Generated on the OAuth App page. Shown only once — store it somewhere safe.

fields.name: base_url
fields.label: GitHub base URL
fields.description: Leave as the default for github.com. Change only if you use GitHub Enterprise Server.
